turns.content: I'm trying to understand the difference between using standard library async traits and the embassy-executor crate for running tasks on a microcontroller.

For that target, you can integrate esp-backtrace alongside embassy-executor by providing a custom panic handler that captures stack traces. The executor itself doesn't include built-in backtracing support, so you'll need to manually configure error handling paths when panics occur in your no_std environment.

I'm trying to get my ESP32 blinker to use embassy-executor instead of the standard library, but I keep getting errors about missing panic handlers and can't seem to make the async tasks actually run on the hardware without a full OS.

That's exactly why you need to initialize the embassy-executor with a custom panic handler before spawning any async work. The executor itself just schedules tasks, but it needs that panic hook to catch fatal errors so your bare-metal system doesn't crash unexpectedly when something goes wrong in the driver code.

Okay, I got the panic handler working, but now my LED isn't blinking at all. The task spawns fine in the executor, but when it tries to use the GPIO peripheral, everything stops and I get a compile error saying the trait bounds aren't met for the async context.

The issue is that embassy-executor requires peripherals to be implemented with specific traits like Send and Sync so they can safely move between threads or run in an async context. You need to ensure your GPIO driver explicitly implements these bounds, otherwise the executor can't guarantee the safety properties required for concurrent execution on a single core.

I checked the code and the traits look correct, but I still can't get it to run. It seems like there's something about how the executor interacts with the task scheduler that I'm missing entirely. How does the executor actually schedule tasks on a microcontroller without an OS?

The embassy-executor uses a simple cooperative multitasking model where it yields control to your async code via the poll loop. Your task must yield back using the yield method every time it needs more CPU cycles, otherwise the executor will never get another chance to run your code. This is different from an OS scheduler which preempts threads.
So I need to manually call poll in my async block? But what if my task needs to do a long delay or read a sensor continuously without yielding?

You can't just ignore the yield requirement. For continuous sensor reading, you should use embassy's built-in timers and channels to create periodic tasks that handle the heavy lifting while keeping your main loop responsive. The executor will still run your timer task in the background using the same cooperative model.
That makes sense, but I'm confused about how embassy handles interrupts and async boundaries together since no_std doesn't have the standard way to do this. Does it use some special trait or macro?

Embassy uses a combination of traits and macros to bridge the gap between bare-metal and async code. Specifically, you need to ensure your peripherals implement the async-aware traits so they can interact with the executor's runtime without breaking safety guarantees.

I think I understand the basics now, but what about error handling? If my async task panics or returns an error, how does the executor deal with it since there's no standard library to catch exceptions?

In a no_std context, you rely entirely on your panic handler and careful trait bounds checking. The executor won't automatically recover from panics in async tasks; instead, it expects you to design your code so that errors are handled gracefully or result in controlled shutdowns.

I've been struggling with this weekend because I thought I could just drop in the executor like a library function. Now I realize it requires careful integration of traits and manual scheduling. Is there any way to simplify this for beginners?

Not really, but starting with simple tasks like LED blinking helps build intuition before moving to complex sensor fusion. The key is understanding that embassy-executor isn't a drop-in replacement; it's a fundamental shift in how you think about concurrency on microcontrollers.

I'm trying to port a legacy application using standard library async traits into our embassy-executor system but the compile errors are telling me about missing Send and Sync bounds on my task type. The documentation says everything should be safe by default, so I don't understand why this is failing.

The issue is that embassy-executor requires the executor itself to implement specific trait bounds for all tasks running on it. Unlike std::task::Context which handles its own memory management, our runtime needs explicit control over task lifetimes and synchronization. You must ensure your custom types implementing the core abstraction actually derive both Send and Sync traits so they can be passed between threads or stored in shared state.

I've already added those derives but it's still complaining about a specific type alias I created for my configuration. The error says this alias doesn't implement the necessary bounds required by the core runtime interface. It seems like just adding derives isn't enough.

When you define a newtype or wrapper type, it inherits whatever traits your inner type has but you must explicitly re-export those bounds in your public API. The problem is likely that you're exposing an internal helper struct that doesn't derive the required safety traits even though the main configuration does. You need to ensure every public type alias used by the core runtime explicitly derives Send and Sync, not just the primary configuration structure.

Ah wait, I see it now. My wrapper struct contains a reference to a dynamic object pool that was only private in my original design but the compiler can't verify its safety unless I make it public with proper bounds. So even though the data is internal, the type system needs full visibility of all traits involved.

Exactly. The runtime checks trait compliance at compile time for every value stored in shared state or passed between tasks. If any field in your configuration structure references a type without proper bounds, the entire wrapper becomes unsafe even if most of it is safe. You need to either make all referenced types public with explicit derives or use owned values instead of references where possible.

That makes sense. I was trying to keep my internal data structures private but the compiler won't let me do that without breaking the safety guarantees required by embassy-executor. So the solution is to either expose all necessary types with proper trait implementations or restructure the code to avoid references entirely.

You can also use the executor's built-in resource management primitives instead of creating your own custom pools if you want to minimize boilerplate. The library provides specialized types for common patterns like thread-safe queues or bounded buffers that already implement the necessary bounds without requiring manual trait derivation.

Perfect, I'll try using those built-in primitives instead of rolling my own implementation. This should resolve the compilation errors immediately since they're designed to be drop-in replacements for standard library equivalents while maintaining full safety guarantees.

Yes, and once you switch to those primitives you'll find that your configuration no longer needs any manual trait bounds because the executor handles all the synchronization internally. The key insight is that in this ecosystem, safety isn't an afterthought—it's baked into every component from the ground up.

I'm reviewing a colleague's code and they're using embassy-executor for async tasks on a microcontroller. I don't see any std library calls, but how does the executor actually handle peripherals that might not be fully initialized yet?

Think of it like a kitchen where some appliances aren't plugged in until you turn them on; the executor is just waiting for those devices to become ready before starting the cooking. In Rust terms, embassy uses the Poll trait and the Executor struct to manage these asynchronous operations without needing the full standard library.
That's helpful, but I'm still confused about how it handles peripherals that aren't fully initialized yet. Can you give me a concrete example of how this works in practice?

Sure, imagine a timer peripheral that only becomes usable after the clock tree is configured. In your code, you'd first initialize the clocks using the embassy-std crate's setup functions, then create an async task that yields to the executor. The executor polls each peripheral individually until they're ready, rather than assuming everything is ready upfront like in a standard library environment.
I see, so it's about polling peripherals one by one? But what if I need to run multiple tasks at once?

Great question. You can use the Executor::spawn method to start multiple async tasks concurrently. Each task runs independently, and when they yield control back to the executor, it switches between them. This is similar to how a thread pool works in standard Rust, but without relying on std::thread or other library features.
Okay, so I can spawn multiple tasks using spawn? But what about error handling? If one task fails, does it affect the others?

You're right to ask. Each spawned task has its own result channel and error handling mechanism. If a task panics or returns an error, it doesn't automatically stop other tasks unless you explicitly handle that case in your design. You can use the Result type from std::result (or no_std equivalents) to manage these outcomes gracefully.
I'm still not clear on how the executor itself is initialized without std. What's the exact sequence of calls needed?

First, you initialize the system using embassy-std::system::init() or similar setup functions depending on your hardware abstraction layer. Then, create an Executor instance and call its start method to begin processing async tasks. This avoids needing any std library initialization because everything is handled through the no_std ecosystem.
What about synchronization between tasks? Like if one task needs data from another?

You can use shared state protected by a mutex or similar synchronization primitive. These types are available in the no_std ecosystem, so you don't need std::sync::Mutex. Just ensure that only one task accesses the shared data at a time to avoid race conditions.
So I can use mutex-like primitives without relying on std? That makes sense, but what about deadlocks?

Deadlocks are still possible if you have circular dependencies between tasks trying to acquire locks. To avoid this, think of it like a relay race where each runner only passes the baton when they're sure the next person is ready. Careful design and testing can prevent these issues.
Thanks for explaining that analogy. One last thing, how do I shut down all tasks cleanly if I need to?

You can use the Executor's shutdown method or gracefully stop individual tasks by sending signals through their result channels. This ensures all pending operations complete before the system exits, similar to how you'd terminate threads in a standard environment but without needing std::thread::sleep.
I'm used to writing async code with tokio and std::future, but I don't understand how embassy-executor works on a microcontroller without std.

The key difference is that embassy-executor provides an executor trait that runs your async tasks directly on the MCU's core without needing a standard library runtime. You don't use spawn or tokio::runtime; instead you implement the embassy::executor::Executor trait to manage task scheduling.
So I just write regular async functions and they get scheduled automatically?

Not exactly. You must explicitly define your tasks using embassy::async_task::Task or similar abstractions so the executor can track them. The executor trait then provides methods like next() to pull the next task off the queue and run it on the CPU.
I thought async tasks would just start running when I called them, similar to how tokio does it with spawn.

That's a common assumption but incorrect in no_std environments. Without std, there's no global runtime thread pool; you must manually manage the lifecycle of each task using the executor trait and ensure cleanup happens when tasks complete or are cancelled.
How do I access peripherals like UART in an async context then?

You use embassy::peripheral modules which provide traits that implement async read/write operations on hardware. For example, you can write to a uart by calling .write() on the peripheral instance and await it, letting the executor handle the blocking.
Does this mean I never use std::pin or std::task::Context?

You don't need std::task::Context because embassy-executor provides its own abstraction for task scheduling and state management. However, you still need to pin your async tasks somewhere since there's no heap or stack frame guaranteed by the runtime.

I'm trying to understand how embassy-executor actually schedules tasks on a microcontroller without an OS. Imagine I have two async peripherals, like a sensor reading and a motor control loop. If I just spawn them with the standard executor, will they truly run concurrently or is one blocking the other? I want to know if the runtime guarantees true parallelism at the hardware level.

The embassy-executor uses a cooperative scheduling model where tasks yield control explicitly. When you spawn async peripherals, they don't block each other unless you force blocking behavior with specific traits like Ready or Yield. The runtime manages the task stack and context switching efficiently, but true parallelism depends on whether your code yields frequently enough to let the scheduler run the next task.

That sounds too easy. I'm worried about real-time constraints where a delay in one peripheral could cause a safety issue. If my sensor task runs for 10 milliseconds, will that actually pause the motor control task? Can embassy-executor guarantee hard deadlines when tasks are long-running?

The executor doesn't provide strict real-time guarantees out of the box because it's designed for cooperative multitasking. If a task runs without yielding, it will monopolize the CPU and delay other tasks indefinitely. You need to ensure every long-running async peripheral explicitly yields using Yield or Ready traits so the scheduler can switch context before deadlines are missed.

So I'm misinterpreting this as a preemptive system? But if I don't yield, my sensor task will starve the motor task. Is there any way to make these tasks truly independent without manual yielding everywhere? Maybe I should use a different pattern like threads or separate stacks?

You cannot achieve true independence without yielding because embassy-executor relies on cooperative scheduling by design. The runtime doesn't preempt other tasks; it only switches when you call Yield or Ready. Using separate stacks helps with isolation but won't solve the starvation problem unless every task yields at critical points.

Okay, so I need to add explicit Yield calls in my sensor and motor tasks. But what about interrupt handling? If an external event triggers a peripheral, will that automatically yield the current task or does it block until the ISR completes?

Interrupts can trigger async peripherals via traits like InterruptHandler, but they don't automatically yield. The ISR runs to completion before returning control back to your main loop. To ensure responsiveness, you must handle interrupts carefully and avoid blocking operations inside them.

I see now that the key is balancing explicit yields with interrupt latency. My sensor task will yield after reading, and my motor task yields before driving. This means neither can run indefinitely without checking for completion. I finally understand how to structure these async peripherals properly.

Exactly. The pattern is to combine explicit yields with careful interrupt handling so that no single peripheral monopolizes the CPU. This cooperative approach lets you build responsive embedded systems without needing an OS kernel.
Why does this even work this way? I thought async only lived in std with tokio, but now we're trying to run async tasks on a microcontroller without the standard library. It feels backwards.

That is exactly why embassy-executor exists; it brings async capabilities into no_std environments where the global allocator and panic hook don't exist. Instead of using the std::task::Context, you create your own executor that manages the task states directly on the stack or heap depending on your memory constraints.
So I'm not using the tokio runtime anymore? Can I just drop the spawn and block functions?

You do not use the tokio runtime; you implement a custom executor that cooperates with the hardware timers. The embassy-executor crate provides the core machinery to manage these async states, allowing peripherals like GPIO or UART to be accessed through async traits without needing any external dependencies.
What about the actual task structure? Does it look like Task in tokio?

The task structure is similar but lightweight; you have a simple enum with variants for running, suspended, and completed states. The executor polls these states using the yield function provided by embassy-async to ensure the MCU stays responsive while waiting for external events.
And what about error handling? I can't use std::result::Result since there's no panic hook?

You handle errors differently; instead of unwrapping or panicking, you return a custom error enum that the executor checks before proceeding. This keeps the system stable even when hardware initialization fails or interrupts occur unexpectedly.
Can I still use the standard library traits like Serialize? What if I need to debug the state?

You can use serde_json::Value for debugging by implementing the Serialize trait manually on your task variants. Since you don't have std::panic, you must ensure all error paths return a safe value that can be logged or stored in non-volatile memory.
Does this mean I lose some of those nice Rust features? Is it really worth the extra complexity?

Yes, you do lose std::panic and some convenience traits, but the trade-off is worth it for performance. The executor avoids unnecessary allocations by managing state directly, which is critical when running on limited resources.
What about the actual implementation details? How does the yield function interact with hardware?

The yield function pauses execution until an interrupt or timer event occurs; it blocks the current task and schedules it for later. The executor uses this to switch between tasks efficiently without context switches that would be too costly on a small MCU.
So I can't just copy-paste tokio code? I have to rewrite everything?

You cannot simply copy-paste tokio code; you must adapt your logic to use embassy-async traits instead. The core idea remains the same—cooperative multitasking—but the implementation details change significantly to fit the no_std constraint.
Hey, I've been reading about no_std async patterns and embassy-executor but still don't get how it actually works on a microcontroller.

Great question. The core idea is that you're running async code without the standard library's runtime, which means you can't use the usual thread spawning or global heap allocation. Instead, embassy-executor manages a specific set of tasks and peripherals directly on the hardware.
So when I say async, does that mean it's still asynchronous in the same way as std::future?

It's very similar but with a critical difference. In no_std contexts like this, you're not using tokio or any standard runtime; instead, embassy provides its own executor trait that cooperatively schedules tasks and handles the async blocks.
Okay, so how do peripherals interact with these async tasks? Are they still just regular functions?

That's the key. Peripherals are wrapped in traits that implement specific embassy abstractions, allowing them to be used within your async code without needing a full operating system or standard library.
Can you give me an example of how I would actually write the code?
You define a custom executor that includes your peripherals, then use embassy's spawn mechanism to start tasks. The runtime will automatically suspend and resume those tasks based on hardware events.
Wait, so I don't need to manually poll the hardware? It seems like magic?
Exactly. The executor handles scheduling and context switching for you based on interrupts and timer events.

I tried to run my code on a microcontroller but it just won't compile. I'm using embassy-executor and the async traits, yet I keep getting errors about missing traits. Why does this even work this way? I expected standard Rust rules to apply here.

The issue is that no_std environments lack core library features like panic hooks and some built-in types. You need to manually implement specific traits for the microcontroller context. Specifically, you must provide an implementation of the trait required by your async runtime so it can interact with hardware peripherals.

But I did exactly what everyone else does in the examples. I implemented the necessary trait and still got errors about missing implementations on my peripheral structs. Why is that happening? Is there something fundamentally different about how these traits are actually used versus standard Rust code?

The problem stems from not understanding how embassy-executor interacts with hardware abstraction layers. Your peripheral structs likely don't implement the required trait methods needed for async operations. You need to ensure every type that needs to be polled or scheduled explicitly provides those trait implementations within your crate's module boundaries.

I checked my code and I did add the necessary functions. But then I read a comment saying embassy-executor is hard because you have to manage state manually instead of relying on the runtime for everything. Can you explain why manual state management breaks things when I try to use it?

Manual state management creates race conditions if not carefully coordinated with the executor's scheduler. The trait system enforces a strict contract where only specific types can be safely polled in parallel contexts. If your implementation violates this by exposing internal state incorrectly, the executor cannot guarantee thread safety even though there are no threads on a microcontroller.

That sounds scary given my limited experience with Rust. I tried to just copy-paste examples but now I'm confused about how to properly structure my code without breaking these rules. Is there a specific pattern I should follow when defining my types?

You should follow the embassy async patterns where you declare your peripheral types and then implement the required trait methods for each one. The key is ensuring all your custom types derive the necessary traits and that your main function properly initializes the executor with these types before any async tasks are spawned.

I implemented the traits but my code still fails to compile. It says some types don't satisfy the requirements. Could you be more specific about what exactly needs to be implemented for common peripherals like LEDs or timers?

For common peripherals, you must implement the trait methods that allow them to be polled. For instance, a timer peripheral typically requires implementing the trait so it can yield control back to the executor after executing its work. Similarly, an LED driver needs these methods to signal completion when toggling states.

I tried adding those implementations but I still get errors about missing functions. Is there something about how embassy-executor actually compiles that differs from normal Rust compilation? Like does it require extra steps before building?

The build process requires careful configuration of your no_std environment and ensuring all dependencies are correctly set up for embedded targets. You must explicitly enable the necessary features in your dependency declarations to activate support for async tasks on microcontrollers.

I added those features but now my code compiles without errors yet it still doesn't run as expected. I'm seeing warnings about unused traits or missing implementations that I thought were covered earlier. Why does this happen if the compiler accepts my code?

The warnings indicate your trait implementations might not fully satisfy the requirements imposed by the runtime environment. Even if the code compiles, the executor may reject certain operations at runtime if the underlying hardware constraints aren't met.

This is getting frustrating. I feel like I'm missing something basic about how these traits actually interact with my specific microcontroller setup. Can you give me a clearer example of what I should do differently?

Consider starting by implementing the trait for your main peripheral type first, ensuring it correctly handles state transitions and yields control appropriately. Then test incrementally to see if adding each implementation resolves the runtime issues.

I did that but now my program just hangs indefinitely instead of executing tasks. It seems like the executor isn't waking up or running anything. What am I missing about how to properly manage execution flow?

The executor needs proper initialization before any async tasks can run. You must ensure your main function correctly sets up the runtime and schedules initial work items so that polling begins immediately.
